* Proposes the **Wealth Thermalization Hypothesis (WTH)**, applying the **Rayleigh-Jeans (RJ) distribution** to model global wealth inequality as a nonlinear dynamical system.
* Maps social stratification layers to **energy levels** while conserving total energy and probability norm, mirroring the **self-cleaning phenomenon** seen in multimode optical fibers.
* Identifies **RJ condensation** as the mechanism creating a bifurcation between a massive "poverty phase" and a highly concentrated "oligarchic phase."
* Demonstrates a **universal description** of wealth distribution validated across GDP, stock market capitalization, and **Bitcoin transactions**.
key_results: Confirmed that the RJ thermal distribution provides a highly accurate fit for real-world Lorenz and Pareto curves.
Identified a dual-phase structure of society consisting of a low-wealth poverty phase and a tiny, dominant oligarchic phase.
Established a mathematical link between constraint-driven condensation in physics and wealth concentration in economics.
Validated the WTH model using diverse financial datasets including the London and Hong Kong stock exchanges.
Showed that thermodynamic principles can be successfully applied to decentralized finance through the analysis of Bitcoin transaction data.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that Rayleigh-Jeans thermal distributions accurately model global wealth inequality and economic stratification patterns.

title: A Neuro-Symbolic Approach to Strategy Synthesis for Strategic Logics
summary: Reasoning about what agents can achieve through strategic interaction is a core challenge in Multi-Agent Systems (MAS). Logics for strategic ability, such as ATL, provide rigorous methods, but their adoption is often hindered by the computational cost of strategy synthesis. We introduce a neuro-symbolic framework that integrates large language models (LLMs) into the model-checking pipeline for MAS. The LLM acts as a strategy-generation oracle, proposing candidate strategies that are then formally validated by a standard MAS model checker. This generate-and-certify architecture uses LLM guidance to navigate large combinatorial strategy spaces while preserving formal soundness: generated strategies are accepted only when certified by the verifier. We instantiate the framework for bounded strategic reasoning in NatATL and introduce the first NatATL strategy-synthesis dataset, consisting of 4211 instances. Experiments with an open-weight Qwen3-32B model show that our certified pipeline achieves 92\% accuracy on strategy-synthesis outcomes.

processed.summary:
- Introduces a **neuro-symbolic framework** for **Multi-Agent Systems (MAS)** that leverages LLMs as strategy-generation oracles.
- Employs a **generate-and-certify architecture** which uses formal MAS model checkers to validate LLM-proposed strategies, ensuring **computational soundness**.
- Targets the **NatATL** logic for bounded strategic reasoning to overcome the high cost of traditional combinatorial strategy synthesis.
- Demonstrates that **local LLMs** (like Qwen3-32B) can effectively navigate large strategy spaces while maintaining formal rigor through validation.
key_results: Achieved 92% accuracy on strategy-synthesis outcomes using a certified neuro-symbolic pipeline.
Introduced the first NatATL strategy-synthesis dataset consisting of 4,211 distinct instances.
Successfully utilized the open-weight Qwen3-32B model to act as a high-performing strategy oracle.
Maintained 100% formal soundness by filtering LLM hallucinations through a symbolic verifier.
Significantly reduced search time by replacing exhaustive combinatorial search with LLM-guided generation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a neuro-symbolic pipeline using LLMs to generate and formally certify strategies in multi-agent systems.

title: Beyond Visual Cues: CoT-Enhanced Reasoning for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Semi-supervised medical image segmentation has emerged as a dominant research problem in medical image analysis, mitigating annotation scarcity by leveraging consistency regularization on unlabeled data. However, existing approaches operate predominantly via visual pattern matching, relying heavily on pixel-level similarities. This visual-centric dependency often falters in clinical scenarios characterized by the visual-semantic mismatch, where visually similar lesions warrant distinct diagnostic conclusions, thus failing to capture the underlying diagnostic logic used by experts. To address this, we move beyond visual cues and propose CERS (CoT-Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation), a framework that integrates Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to distinguish pathologically distinct cases. Specifically, we construct a knowledge pool enriched with linguistic reasoning descriptions generated by large language models (LLMs). A semantic-aware reference selection strategy is introduced to identify historical evidence, filtering candidates first by morphology, and then refining them via CoT consistency to eliminate hard negatives. Furthermore, a multi-scale coordinate attention module (MCAM) is designed to effectively fuse this reasoning-derived context into the decoding process. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of CERS against state-of-the-art approaches, particularly in resolving boundary ambiguities and semantic inconsistencies. The code is available at https://github.com/cymasuna/CERS.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **CERS (CoT-Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation)**, a framework that integrates **Chain-of-Thought reasoning** into semi-supervised medical image segmentation to resolve visual-semantic mismatches.
- Employs a **knowledge pool** of LLM-generated linguistic reasoning descriptions to capture diagnostic logic that simple pixel-level pattern matching misses.
- Features a **semantic-aware reference selection** strategy that filters candidates by morphology and refines them using **CoT consistency** to eliminate hard negatives.
- Implements a **Multi-scale Coordinate Attention Module (MCAM)** to fuse reasoning-derived linguistic context into the visual decoding process for better boundary accuracy.
key_results: Surpasses state-of-the-art semi-supervised methods by leveraging diagnostic logic over pure visual matching.
Successfully mitigates the visual-semantic mismatch where similar lesions require different diagnostic labels.
Improves segmentation performance specifically in areas of high boundary ambiguity and semantic inconsistency.
Demonstrates that LLM-derived reasoning can be effectively distilled into pixel-level computer vision tasks.
Provides an open-source implementation for integrating reasoning models into medical imaging workflows.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: CERS Research Team introduces a framework using Chain-of-Thought reasoning to improve medical image segmentation by resolving visual-semantic mismatches.

title: Revisiting quantum effects on dislocation glide in bcc metals from DFT calculations and machine-learning potentials
summary: Quantum zero-point effects have been long proposed to explain a well-known discrepancy between the low-temperature flow stresses of body-centered cubic metals and corresponding atomistic models of plastic flow. Previous investigations on quantum effects relied on empirical interatomic potentials, which poorly reproduce dislocation energy landscapes compared to density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Here, we revisit this problem using DFT and machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs). We show that while quantum effects do contribute to dislocation glide at low temperature, their magnitude is much lower than previously reported, and insufficient to reconcile atomistic predictions with experiments. Our results thus reopen a long-standing question and challenge for predictive atomistic modeling, on a fundamental property of crystals.

• Evaluates the impact of **quantum zero-point effects** on dislocation glide, revealing that previous models significantly overestimated their contribution.

title: MLLMs Get It Right, Then Get It Wrong: Tracing and Correcting Late-Layer Textual Bias
summary: When vision contradicts text, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) consistently favor text, even when images provide clear evidence otherwise. This bias poses risks for applications requiring visual grounding, yet its cause remains unclear. In this paper, we uncover a surprising finding: models often get it right initially, forming correct vision-based predictions in their intermediate layers, before changing their minds and favoring text in the final output. We call this "late-layer textual override". The visual information is encoded, it simply does not survive to the output. More intriguingly, we find that how predictions change reveals whether they're correct: 85% of failures shift toward text, while 89% of successes shift toward vision. This directional signature enables a simple but powerful intervention: when we detect a confident visual prediction being suppressed, we restore it. We propose CALRD (Conflict-Aware Layer Reference Decoding), a training-free method that recovers overridden predictions at inference time. Experiments across five MLLMs of varying architectures demonstrate up to 9.4% absolute improvements on conflict benchmarks while largely preserving standard performance, without training or external knowledge. It recovers what the model already knew but failed to preserve.

processed.summary:
* Researchers identify **late-layer textual override**, a phenomenon where MLLMs correctly process visual data in intermediate layers but default to textual priors in the final output layers.
* The study introduces **Conflict-Aware Layer Reference Decoding (CALRD)**, a training-free inference method that restores visual predictions suppressed by the model's language-biased final layers.
* The approach leverages a **directional signature** in layer transitions, noting that 85% of model failures occur when predictions shift from vision-based to text-based in late stages.
* By monitoring layer-wise evolution, CALRD achieves up to **9.4% absolute improvement** on conflict benchmarks without requiring external knowledge or additional training.
key_results: Identified that correct visual representations exist in intermediate layers but are overridden by textual bias in final layers.
Discovered that 85% of failures shift toward text, while 89% of successes shift toward vision-consistent outputs.
Developed CALRD, a training-free inference-time intervention to recover suppressed visual knowledge.
Achieved up to 9.4% absolute improvement on multimodal conflict benchmarks.
Demonstrated performance gains across five different MLLM architectures without compromising standard benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CALRD to correct late-layer textual bias in MLLMs, improving visual grounding through inference-time layer monitoring.

title: SoftMoE: Soft Differentiable Routing for Mixture-of-Experts in LLMs
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures enable scaling LLM parameters under a fixed inference budget by activating only a small subset of experts via top-$k$ routing. While this preserves causality and suits autoregressive language models, the discrete top-$k$ operator is not differentiable, forcing a fixed number of active experts per input and resulting in inefficient use of computation. We propose SoftMoE, which replaces discrete routing with a truncated soft top-$k$ LapSum relaxation, allowing gradient-based optimization of expert routing. We further parameterize the mean number of active experts per layer and impose a global budget constraint, enabling the model to learn how to allocate expert capacity across layers. SoftMoE remains fully compatible with autoregressive modeling and achieves performance comparable to or better than sparse MoE on language modeling and downstream tasks, while activating significantly fewer experts. Notably, the learned allocation is highly non-uniform, with later layers activating more experts. The source code is publicly available$^\dagger$.

* Introduces **SoftMoE**, an architecture that replaces traditional discrete top-k routing with a **differentiable soft relaxation** using a LapSum operator.
* Enables **gradient-based optimization** of expert selection, allowing the model to learn the most efficient routing paths during training.
* Features a **global budget constraint** that permits the system to dynamically allocate expert capacity non-uniformly across transformer layers.
* Maintains full compatibility with **autoregressive language modeling** while improving parameter efficiency and inference performance.
key_results: Achievement of performance equal to or better than sparse MoE with fewer active experts.
Successfully addressed the non-differentiability of discrete top-k operators in MoE routing.
Discovery that optimal expert allocation is non-uniform, with later layers requiring more experts.
Implementation of a global budget constraint that optimizes compute across the entire model.
Reduction in computational overhead during inference compared to standard sparse MoE models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce SoftMoE, a differentiable routing method that optimizes expert allocation across layers for more efficient LLM scaling.

title: Data-Driven Discovery of a Simple Phantom-Crossing Dark Energy Parametrization
summary: We develop a data-driven reconstruction programme for the dark-energy equation of state within VCDM, a minimally modified gravity framework in which both background and linear perturbations can be consistently evolved across the phantom divide. Using CMB, BAO, and type-Ia supernova data, we first perform a Bayesian spline reconstruction of $w(a)$, finding a preference for smooth, monotonic phantom-crossing trajectories. Bayesian evidence disfavors increasingly complex spline models, indicating that current observations exhibit a statistical preference for low-complexity dark-energy dynamics. Motivated by this result, we apply Exhaustive Symbolic Regression, an interpretable machine-learning technique that systematically searches over analytic expressions of fixed complexity, identifying the remarkably simple one-parameter form $w(a)={w_0}/{\sqrt a}$, which reproduces the reconstructed behaviour and fits the data at a level comparable to standard two-parameter parametrizations such as CPL. The model naturally crosses the phantom divide for $w_0<0$, suppresses early dark energy, and predicts a transient accelerating and phantom phase without a future big-rip singularity. As a one-parameter model, it is highly predictive, being a genuinely dynamical deformation of the cosmological constant rather than containing it as a limit. Bayesian model comparison yields mild-to-moderate support for this parametrization relative to standard two-parameter alternatives, and stronger evidence relative to $Λ$CDM. Our results suggest that current observations favour surprisingly simple dark-energy dynamics and illustrate how Bayesian reconstruction and symbolic regression can be combined into a principled model-discovery framework for cosmology.

- Develops a **Bayesian spline reconstruction** pipeline using CMB, BAO, and supernova data to identify smooth, monotonic phantom-crossing trajectories in the VCDM framework.
- Introduces a **one-parameter parametrization** (w(a) = w0 / sqrt(a)) that simplifies dark energy modeling while maintaining predictive accuracy comparable to complex two-parameter alternatives.
key_results: Identified a highly predictive one-parameter model for the dark energy equation of state.
Validated that current cosmological data statistically favors low-complexity dark energy dynamics over complex splines.
Model successfully crosses the phantom divide without future 'big-rip' singularities.

Demonstrated a principled model-discovery framework combining Bayesian reconstruction with symbolic machine learning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize Exhaustive Symbolic Regression to discover a simple one-parameter dark energy model that outperforms standard cosmological benchmarks.

title: RouteBalance: Fused Model Routing and Load Balancing for Heterogeneous LLM Serving
summary: Heterogeneous LLM serving stacks split scheduling into two layers that optimize in isolation: model routers pick a model from quality and cost signals while ignoring instance load, and serving load balancers optimize queues while ignoring quality. We present RouteBalance, a serving-aware scheduling layer that fuses both into a single online assignment over concrete model instances, jointly trading off quality, latency, and cost. A batched in-process predictor stack and dead-reckoned instance state keep the joint decision cheap on the request hot path ($\approx$32 ms at 12 req/s). On a 13-instance, 28-GPU heterogeneous cluster serving four model sizes, a single deployed RouteBalance stack traces the upper region of the three-way quality-cost-throughput frontier. Sweeping one weight vector reaches both the highest routing-decision quality (DeepEval $0.419$, $+0.013$ over the strongest baseline, $95\%$ CI $[{+}0.005,{+}0.022]$; the ordering holds when a second judge re-scores the actually served text) and, at its cost-priority corner, per-request cost that ties the cheapest baseline. With router engineering equalized against concurrent-scoring baseline variants we build, its balanced preset serves at $2.8$ s and $30$ req/s, leading $2.6$ to $4.1\times$ ahead of enhanced BEST-Route at high load. (Deploying those routers as published, one serial scoring call per request, makes them collapse $23\times$ under load, a deployment-architecture effect we isolate separately, not the routing result.) A four-arm isolation shows the benefit follows from pricing latency at model-selection time; the learned predictors contribute calibration and SLO headroom rather than the headline frontier. Code: https://github.com/AKafakA/route-balance

- Implements a **batched in-process predictor stack** and dead-reckoned instance state to maintain low-latency decision-making (~32 ms overhead) on the request hot path.
- Targets **heterogeneous clusters** by jointly optimizing the trade-offs between response quality, serving latency, and operational cost.
- Addresses the throughput collapse observed in traditional serial-scoring routers by using a deployment-aware architecture designed for high-load environments.

Outperformed enhanced BEST-Route by 2.6x to 4.1x in throughput during high-load scenarios.
Successfully tied the cheapest baseline for per-request cost when configured for cost-priority.

Isolated the benefit of pricing latency at model-selection time to improve SLO headroom.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: RouteBalance researchers introduce a fused scheduling layer that jointly optimizes quality, cost, and latency for heterogeneous LLM clusters.

title: Small Initialization Matters for Large Language Models
summary: Large language models provide a tractable system for asking how intelligence itself emerges, rather than only how LLMs can be engineered. Although progress is usually attributed to scale, data and architecture, we show that parameter initialization is a gene-like determinant of training and, in particular, of model capacity. Reducing the initialization scale consistently improves pretraining, with the largest gains on reasoning-demanding tasks. We identify two widely used empirical settings that restrain the advantage of small initialization, and show how relaxing them restores favorable scaling. We further uncover a critical initialization that balances the reasoning and training. Mechanistically, small initialization drives a distinct developmental trajectory: parameters first condense into low-complexity structures and later expand into richer representations, giving concrete form to the idea that compression is intelligence. Token-level analyses show that the gains concentrate on non-trivial, context-constrained predictions rather than all tokens uniformly. These results motivate a simple $γ$-initialization rule: expose initialization rage as an explicit knob and use small initialization by default, an almost cost-free intervention that improves pretraining and strengthens reasoning across model scales.

- Introduces the **gamma-initialization rule**, recommending that the initialization range be exposed as an explicit hyperparameter and set to small values by default.
- Demonstrates that gains are non-uniform, concentrating specifically on **context-constrained predictions** and reasoning tasks rather than simple token sequences.

The most significant performance gains are observed in reasoning-demanding benchmarks.
Identified and addressed specific empirical training settings that previously masked the benefits of small initialization.

title: PepsiCo Deploys AI-Driven Pricing and Promotion Optimization at Scale
summary:
Effective pricing and promotion planning constitutes a central pillar of strategic revenue management for firms operating in highly competitive and dynamic markets. These planning activities require the simultaneous consideration of demand elasticity, competitor actions, channel and market specific constraints, and financial objectives. As the dimensionality and interdependencies inherent in these problems increase, manual or traditional approaches become suboptimal and insufficient. In this context, Operations Research provides a robust methodological foundation for scalable, data-driven decision support systems that can optimize complex planning processes across large product and customer portfolios.
  This paper presents two large-scale optimization systems developed and deployed at PepsiCo to support Revenue Growth Management initiatives: PromoAI and PricingAI. PromoAI couples machine learning-based promotional forecasts with a mixed-integer linear programming model to optimize promotional calendars across trade channels, searching millions of product-promotion-timing combinations for the one that maximizes PepsiCo and retailer revenues subject to customizable business constraints. PricingAI optimizes base prices across product portfolios over multi-period horizons, using Bayesian hierarchical models to estimate own- and cross-price elasticities and competitive interactions, then feeding these into a nonlinear programming engine that recommends price changes aligned with revenue and margin targets under operational constraints.
  Together, these systems demonstrate the feasibility and scalability of advanced optimization in large-scale enterprise environments. They highlight the value of integrating statistical learning with mathematical programming to enable enterprise-level, automated decision-making that is both data-informed and aligned with strategic business objectives.

• **PromoAI** architecture couples machine learning-based forecasting with a **Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP)** model to optimize promotional calendars across millions of product combinations.
• **PricingAI** utilizes **Bayesian hierarchical models** to estimate own- and cross-price elasticities, feeding a nonlinear programming engine for multi-period price optimization.
• The systems integrate **mathematical programming** with statistical learning to satisfy complex business constraints and financial objectives at an enterprise scale.
• This deployment demonstrates the feasibility of combining high-dimensional **Operations Research** techniques with ML for automated, data-driven revenue management.
key_results: Development and deployment of PromoAI for large-scale promotional calendar optimization.
Implementation of PricingAI for base price optimization using Bayesian elasticity estimation.
Capability to search millions of product-promotion-timing combinations to maximize revenue.
Successful integration of competitor interactions and cross-price elasticities into decision engines.
Automated alignment of tactical pricing decisions with strategic enterprise margin targets.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: PepsiCo deploys PromoAI and PricingAI to optimize revenue growth through machine learning and large-scale mathematical programming.

title: ThinkingVLA: Interleaved Vision and Language Reasoning for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Most Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models map observations directly to actions without explicit reasoning, limiting their capacity for reasoning-intensive long-horizon tasks. To address this, existing approaches adopt Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to enable subgoal decomposition and spatial anticipation. However, those methods lack a unified architecture for effective cross-modal reasoning and fail to explicitly include inverse reasoning ability based on the target state. We argue that manipulation planning naturally decomposes into prediction, anticipating the next visual state, and inverse dynamics, inferring the actions to reach it. Bridging both requires a unified autoregressive architecture that interleaves textual and visual reasoning in a single generation process. We propose \textbf{ThinkingVLA}, a generative model that realizes this decomposition within a unified Mixture-of-Transformers architecture. ThinkingVLA consists of a forward CoT that identifies the immediate subgoal and guides the visual forecasting; the predicted image then serves as the target state, grounding an inverse CoT that reasons about spatial relationships and action intent based on the predicted image; and the final action is generated conditioned on this full reasoning context. Extensive experiments on simulation and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that ThinkingVLA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with particularly large gains on long-horizon manipulation tasks.

* Introduces **ThinkingVLA**, a generative framework utilizing a **Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT)** architecture to integrate text-based reasoning with visual forecasting.
* Implements a dual-stage reasoning process consisting of a **Forward Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** for subgoal identification and an **Inverse CoT** for spatial/intent reasoning based on predicted images.
* Unifies prediction and inverse dynamics within a single **autoregressive generation process**, allowing the model to ground actions in anticipated future visual states.
* Specifically addresses the limitations of standard VLA models in **long-horizon tasks** by explicitly modeling the transition between current and target visual states.
key_results: ThinkingVLA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art VLA baselines in both simulation and real-world settings.
Significant performance improvements were observed in long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks.
The model effectively bridges textual reasoning and visual state prediction in a unified architecture.
Validation shows that grounding inverse CoT in predicted future images improves spatial reasoning accuracy.
Mixture-of-Transformers architecture demonstrates robust handling of interleaved vision and language reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ThinkingVLA researchers introduce a Mixture-of-Transformers model that interleaves visual forecasting and dual-stage reasoning for enhanced robotic manipulation.

title: PreAct: Computer-Using Agents that Get Faster on Repeated Tasks
summary:
Computer-using agents drive real software through the screen -- clicking and typing -- but they solve every task from scratch: asked to repeat a task, an agent re-reads the screen, re-reasons every tap, and pays the full cost again. We present PreAct, which lets such an agent get faster on tasks it has done before. The first time it succeeds, PreAct compiles the run into a small state-machine program-states that check the screen, transitions that act-and on later runs replays it directly instead of invoking the agent 8.5-13x faster, with no per-step language-model calls. Replay is not blind: at each step PreAct checks that the screen matches what the program expects before acting, and hands control back to the agent the moment something is off.
  PreAct applies the same discipline when deciding what to keep: a freshly compiled program enters the store only if, re-run from a clean state, an independent evaluator confirms it solved the task-catching programs that replay to their last step yet leave the task undone. Across a mobile, a desktop, and a web benchmark, this store-time check separates repeated runs that improve from ones that degrade as faulty programs accumulate, worth 1.75-2.6 tasks per benchmark, the same direction on all three; a fallback that explores afresh when no program fits brings PreAct level with a strong record-and-replay baseline. We also report what did not matter: prompt wording, runtime guardrails, and whether a language model or a plain embedding retriever selects which program to reuse.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **PreAct**, a framework that compiles successful agent trajectories into small **state-machine programs** to enable efficient re-execution on repeated tasks.
- Implements a **hybrid execution model** where the agent replays compiled programs 8.5-13x faster by eliminating per-step LLM calls and checking screen states against expected transitions.
- Features a rigorous **store-time validation** process where an independent evaluator verifies task completion from a clean state before committing programs to memory.
- Includes a **robust fallback mechanism** that triggers fresh exploration when no stored program matches the current environment, maintaining reliability across mobile, desktop, and web benchmarks.
key_results: Achieved 8.5-13x faster execution speeds on repeated tasks compared to standard agent reasoning loops.
Eliminated the need for per-step language model calls during successful program re-runs.
Store-time validation improved benchmark scores by 1.75-2.6 tasks by filtering out faulty programs.
Demonstrated that program selection accuracy is driven by validation rather than specific prompt wording or retriever type.
Maintained performance parity with strong record-and-replay baselines through an automated exploration fallback.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce PreAct, which compiles agent trajectories into state-machines for 13x faster, LLM-free task repetition and execution.

title: Parasitic Masquerade: Societal Scale Human-Machine Interaction
summary: This work extends recent developments in studying human--machine interaction by scaling from individual game-theoretic models to a societal-level model. We adopt a Graphon Mean-Field Game (GMFG) that models the interaction among four groups of internally-homogeneous but externally-heterogeneous agents in a shared environment. Our results show that parasitism can masquerade as productive learning, with knowledge distribution and actions appearing healthy while being driven by machine coupling rather than independent investigation. To detect this, we measure the direction of information flow and belief entropy of the environment, revealing that human to machine channel dominates across all scenarios, with the asymmetry intensifying under parasitism. We further demonstrate that the system exhibits coexisting mutualistic and parasitic equilibria, where environmental noise can induce a tipping point that shifts agents past the cognitive cost barrier. These emergent phenomena are not designed into any individual agent but arise from the collective interaction structure, underscoring the need to study the sociology of humans and machines holistically as a complex system.

- Identifies **parasitic masquerade**, a state where machine-driven coupling mimics productive learning while actually eroding independent human investigation.
- Introduces **information flow direction** and **belief entropy** as critical metrics to distinguish between mutualistic and parasitic societal equilibria.
- Explores how **environmental noise** acts as a catalyst for tipping points, shifting the system across cognitive cost barriers into emergent collective states.

title: PearlVLA: Progressive Embodied Action-Plan Refinement in Latent Space
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face a trade-off between efficient action generation and explicit deliberation. Directly decoding actions from vision-language backbone representations enables low-latency control, whereas explicit reasoning through textual chains, pixel-level subgoals, or action search can improve planning but incurs substantial latency and computational cost. We propose PearlVLA, a VLA framework that moves deliberation into the latent space of a vision-language model (VLM). PearlVLA separates VLM meta-query representations into a fixed visual grounding branch and an iterative latent plan branch. At each refinement round, a plan-conditioned world query probes a lightweight frozen latent world model for an action-free future observation latent, which is fed back to guide plan refinement. A future-guided RefineNet then applies scheduled residual updates to progressively refine a coarse semantic draft into a fine-grained latent action plan. The refined plan after K rounds is then decoded in parallel into an action chunk for low-latency execution. We further introduce Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL to optimize the latent refinement process with rewards from longer-horizon imagined futures induced by latent plan edits. Empirical evaluations on the LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that PearlVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance among existing methods.

- The architecture separates representations into a **fixed visual grounding branch** and an **iterative latent plan branch**, utilizing a frozen latent world model to probe future states.
- A **future-guided RefineNet** applies scheduled residual updates to progressively transform coarse semantic drafts into fine-grained latent action plans.
- The system utilizes **Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL** to optimize the refinement process using rewards derived from imagined future trajectories.

Eliminated the high latency typically associated with textual or pixel-level explicit reasoning.
Enabled parallel decoding of action chunks for low-latency, high-frequency robot control.
Demonstrated that latent-space edits can effectively guide plan refinement via world model queries.

processed.one_sentence_takeaway: PearlVLA researchers introduce progressive latent-space refinement to enable high-speed, high-reasoning embodied AI actions.

title: On Injectivity of Phase Retrieval
summary: In this short note, we prove that if $A \in \mathbb C^{N \times M}$ with $N=4M-5$ has i.i.d.\ standard complex Gaussian entries, then the probability that the phase retrieval map generated by $A$ is not injective is positive. This proves Part (1) of a conjecture of Cynthia Vinzant, which was later restated by Afonso S. Bandeira in \cite{BDL+26}. The main result of this paper was obtained using generative AI, in particular the Rethlas system.

processed.summary:
- The paper provides a formal proof concerning the **injectivity of phase retrieval** maps, specifically focusing on complex Gaussian matrices where N=4M-5.
- A significant breakthrough is the methodology, as the core result was obtained using the **Rethlas system**, a specialized **generative AI** tool for mathematical reasoning.
- This work confirms a long-standing conjecture by Cynthia Vinzant and Afonso S. Bandeira, demonstrating the power of **AI-assisted research** in theoretical mathematics.
key_results: Proves Part (1) of the Vinzant conjecture regarding phase retrieval injectivity.
Establishes that for N=4M-5, the probability of a non-injective map is positive.
Utilizes standard complex Gaussian entries to validate theoretical bounds.
Demonstrates the successful application of the Rethlas AI system in formal proof discovery.
Refines the mathematical boundaries of signal reconstruction in complex dimensions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers leverage the Rethlas generative AI system to solve a complex mathematical conjecture on phase retrieval injectivity.

title: OlfactProfile: Profile-Conditioned Odor Prediction from Audiovisual Content
summary:
Automated video-odor matching predicts scents aligned with audiovisual content for scent-enhanced media. Existing methods usually treat odor labels as determined only by scene content, but odor judgment also depends on individual olfactory profiles, including scent sensitivity, tolerance to unpleasant odors, and affective preference. Ignoring this observer context limits current systems' ability to predict scents that match perceived experience.
  We present OlfactProfile, a framework for profile-conditioned odor prediction from audiovisual content. Our results show that olfactory profiles are not beneficial by default: with matched feature backbones, naive profile concatenation and uniform profile modulation can degrade performance, while structured field-wise profile conditioning consistently improves prediction. Thus, the key challenge is not merely whether observer context is available, but how it is integrated into multimodal reasoning.
  To study this setting, we construct an audiovisual benchmark pairing temporally aligned odor annotations with annotator olfactory preference profiles. It contains 1,350 video clips, a 99-class scent vocabulary, and three semantic odor tracks: Foreground Odor, Background Odor, and Emotion Odor. We also propose OAR (Olfactory-Aware Routing), a multimodal fusion module that performs track-aware audiovisual routing with field-wise profile modulation, allowing profile dimensions to influence odor reasoning according to perceptual role. Experiments show that OlfactProfile outperforms supervised baselines and general-purpose multimodal large models, is competitive with odor experts in a small human comparison, and improves perceived scent fit in scent-enhanced applications without task-specific fine-tuning. Per-track analysis shows that gains are strongest for Background Odor and Emotion Odor, where observer-dependent judgment is most important.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **OlfactProfile**, a framework for personalized odor prediction from audiovisual data by integrating **individual olfactory profiles** (sensitivity, tolerance, and preference).
* Proposes **Olfactory-Aware Routing (OAR)**, a multimodal fusion module that utilizes **field-wise profile modulation** to influence odor reasoning based on the perceptual role of the observer context.
* Addresses the failure of naive profile integration by demonstrating that structured conditioning is necessary to avoid performance degradation in multimodal reasoning.
* Validated on a new benchmark of 1,350 video clips across three semantic tracks—**Foreground, Background, and Emotion Odor**—outperforming general-purpose MLLMs.
key_results: Naive profile concatenation and uniform modulation often degrade performance compared to no profile.
Field-wise profile conditioning consistently improves prediction accuracy across all odor tracks.
The framework is most effective for Background and Emotion odors where observer subjectivity is highest.
OlfactProfile demonstrates performance competitive with human odor experts in zero-shot comparisons.
The 99-class scent vocabulary benchmark provides a new standard for multimodal scent-enhanced media research.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: OlfactProfile researchers introduce a multimodal framework that uses field-wise profile conditioning to predict personalized odors from video content.

title: Trustworthy Self-Composable Big-Data-as-a-Service: An LLM-Orchestrated Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Data Engineering, AutoML, MLOps Deployment, and Drift-Aware Lifecycle Optimization
summary: Big-Data-as-a-Service (BDaaS) platforms require re liable automation across data ingestion, cleaning, feature engi neering, model development, deployment, and post-deployment monitoring. However, existing LLM-based data science agents and AutoML systems mainly focus on isolated workflow stages, leaving limited support for lifecycle-level orchestration, artifact governance, human oversight, and drift-aware adaptation. This paper proposes a trustworthy self-composable BDaaS frame work based on LLM-orchestrated multi-agent collaboration. The proposed architecture decomposes the BDaaS lifecycle into specialized agents for data ingestion, data cleaning, feature engineering, AutoML training, model evaluation, MLOps de ployment, monitoring, and drift detection. A central LLM or chestration layer coordinates agent execution, validates interme diate outputs, manages workflow context, and enables dynamic workflow composition. The framework also incorporates shared artifact governance, reproducibility support, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and drift-aware feedback loops. A prototype-based evaluation is conducted using controlled tabular benchmark datasets with missing values, categorical variables, outliers, class imbalance, and simulated covariate drift. Compared with manual ML, AutoML-only, and single-agent LLM baselines, the pro posed multi-agent BDaaS pipeline achieves competitive predictive performance while improving lifecycle-level reliability, including workflow completion, artifact traceability, deployment readiness, reproducibility, and drift recovery. The results suggest that LLM-orchestrated multi-agent systems can extend conventional AutoML toward trustworthy, adaptive, and production-oriented BDaaS lifecycle automation.

* The framework introduces a **central LLM orchestration layer** that coordinates specialized agents for data ingestion, cleaning, feature engineering, and MLOps tasks.
* It moves beyond isolated AutoML by implementing **drift-aware feedback loops** and **shared artifact governance** to ensure end-to-end lifecycle reliability.
* The architecture supports **dynamic workflow composition**, allowing for flexible adaptation to data quality issues like missing values, outliers, and class imbalance.
* **Human-in-the-loop checkpoints** and reproducibility support are integrated to bridge the gap between automated experimentation and production-ready deployment.

Significantly improved workflow completion rates for complex, multi-stage data engineering tasks.
Demonstrated robust drift recovery capabilities through automated monitoring and feedback-driven retraining.

Streamlined deployment readiness by automating the transition from AutoML training to MLOps monitoring.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a multi-agent BDaaS framework that automates the full machine learning lifecycle with drift-aware orchestration.

title: ChLogic: Evaluating Robustness of Logical Reasoning in Chinese Expressions
summary: Large language models perform increasingly well on standardized logical reasoning benchmarks, but whether this ability remains robust beyond English is unclear. We introduce ChLogic, an English--Chinese aligned benchmark that tests whether models preserve logical reasoning performance when the same latent logical structure is expressed in English and diverse Chinese surface realizations. Built from formal logical templates, the benchmark contains three data sets: (i) the General aligned set, derived from 60 General Propositions across nine template families; (ii) the Difficult aligned set, derived from 40 Difficult Problems; and (iii) the Chinese-only set, covering 15 language-specific phenomenon types. Each aligned item pairs one English reference expression with five Chinese realizations. Experiments on Qwen3, Ministral, and GLM models reveal a persistent English--Chinese performance gap. Back-translation from standard Chinese into English often improves performance on the General aligned set, but produces mixed effects on the Difficult aligned set, where Qwen3-32B and GLM-5.1 perform worse after translation. These results indicate that Chinese surface realization, translation artifacts, and model-specific behavior jointly affect multilingual logical reasoning. Overall, ChLogic provides a useful stress test for the robustness of multilingual reasoning.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **ChLogic**, an evaluation benchmark designed to assess the robustness of **multilingual logical reasoning** by aligning English and Chinese expressions of the same logical structures.
* The framework incorporates three distinct datasets: a **General aligned set**, a **Difficult aligned set**, and a **Chinese-only set** focusing on language-specific logical phenomena.
* Evaluations on **Qwen3**, **Ministral**, and **GLM** models demonstrate a persistent performance gap when logical structures are presented in Chinese versus English.
* The study investigates **model-specific behaviors** regarding back-translation, finding that while it helps with general propositions, it can degrade reasoning in complex scenarios.
key_results: Models consistently perform better on English reasoning tasks than their Chinese counterparts with identical logic.
Back-translation into English improves scores for simpler general propositions across most tested models.
Advanced models like Qwen3-32B and GLM-5.1 showed decreased performance on difficult reasoning sets after translation.
Chinese-specific linguistic phenomena and surface realizations pose unique challenges for current LLM reasoning capabilities.
The benchmark identifies that translation artifacts and surface realizations are primary drivers of reasoning inconsistency.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that Chinese surface realizations significantly degrade logical reasoning performance in LLMs compared to English counterparts.

title: Dynamic Rollout Editing for Reducing Overthinking in RL-Trained Reasoning Models
summary: Long-form chain-of-thought reasoning can improve LLM performance on complex tasks, but models often continue generating unnecessary reasoning after a correct answer has emerged. We refer to this behavior as overthinking. We study this phenomenon from the perspective of GRPO-style reinforcement learning (RL) post-training, framing it as a training-time credit-assignment problem rather than merely a decoding-time stopping problem. In rollouts sampled at the onset of GRPO training, we observe that successful trajectories can exhibit a slightly higher degree of overthinking than unsuccessful trajectories for the same prompts. This early imbalance provides a starting point for an undesirable feedback loop: because GRPO assigns sequence-level credit, it cannot distinguish the solution-reaching prefix from the unnecessary continuation that lengthens a successful trajectory. Both receive positive update signal, allowing the initial imbalance to grow into more severe overthinking during training. To address this issue, we introduce Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), a training-time intervention for successful trajectories that continue thinking after answer emergence. DRE preserves the accepted verified prefix, edits the remaining thinking, and prefers the edited trajectory within the same RL group, weakening the preference signal for unnecessary thinking without penalizing the reasoning needed to reach the answer. Experiments across diverse tasks show the effectiveness of DRE.

processed.summary:
- Identifies a **credit-assignment problem** in **GRPO-style reinforcement learning** where sequence-level rewards reinforce unnecessary reasoning generated after a correct answer is reached.
- Proposes **Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE)**, a training-time intervention that preserves the verified solution prefix while pruning or editing superfluous "overthinking" segments.
- Breaks the **undesirable feedback loop** where early training imbalances lead to increasingly verbose and inefficient chain-of-thought trajectories.
- Improves **inference efficiency** by reducing token count and latency without degrading the model's reasoning performance or accuracy.
key_results: Observed that successful early-training trajectories are often more verbose than unsuccessful ones.
DRE effectively decouples solution-reaching logic from redundant post-answer computations.
Successfully reduced 'overthinking' behavior across diverse complex reasoning benchmarks.
Maintained performance parity with standard GRPO while significantly lowering token usage.
Demonstrated that sequence-level credit assignment is a primary driver of reasoning model bloat.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Dynamic Rollout Editing to prune redundant reasoning in RL models, improving efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.

title: MathVis-Fine: Aligning Visual Supervision with Necessity via Progressive Dependency-Guided Training for Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has extended from purely linguistic domains to multimodal scenarios; however, existing approaches often treat visual inputs as homogeneous or auxiliary signals, failing to capture the intricate and sample-specific dependencies between text and images in mathematical problem-solving. This gives rise to two core issues: first, the supervisory signals for visual content are generalized and coarse-grained, lacking adaptation to the actual necessity of visual information in each sample; second, training feedback becomes inaccurate when visual rewards are uniformly applied without distinguishing the complementary relationships among inputs. These limitations hinder models from achieving precise multimodal reasoning. In this work, we propose a framework for modeling fine-grained visual dependencies in mathematical reasoning. We first construct the MathVis-Fine dataset, augmenting fine-grained visual annotations with visual dependency ratings. Building upon this dataset, we introduce a two-stage progressive visual enhancement training paradigm that balances answer correctness rewards and visual grounding rewards according to the intrinsic visual dependency level of each sample, thereby mitigating reward bias and improving supervision accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the MathVis-Fine framework effectively enhances visual perception progressively based on visual dependency, offering a more precise training framework for multimodal mathematical reasoning. We will release the dataset upon acceptance.

processed.summary:
• Introduces the **MathVis-Fine dataset**, which provides fine-grained visual annotations and **dependency ratings** to quantify the necessity of visual information in mathematical contexts.
• Proposes a **two-stage progressive training paradigm** that balances answer correctness with **visual grounding rewards** based on sample-specific dependency levels.
• Mitigates **supervisory noise** and reward bias by distinguishing between complementary and redundant visual-text relationships.
• Enhances the precision of **multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** reasoning by aligning visual supervision with the actual complexity of the input.
key_results: Created the MathVis-Fine dataset featuring nuanced visual-text dependency labels.
Implemented a two-stage framework for progressive visual perception enhancement.
Reduced reward bias by dynamically weighting grounding versus correctness signals.
Achieved superior reasoning performance on multimodal mathematical benchmarks.
Validated that sample-specific dependency modeling improves training stability and accuracy.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MathVis-Fine to optimize multimodal reasoning through progressive, dependency-guided training and fine-grained visual supervision.

title: AI Adoption Across a Multinational Workforce: Sociotechnical Conditions for GenAI Acceptance in Human Resources
summary: Generative AI (GenAI) deployment in the workplace is accelerating rapidly. Nevertheless, questions of who adopts, who benefits, and who is left behind and why are still understudied. In this paper, we investigate these dynamics in the context of a multinational tech company transitioning from a legacy Human Resources (HR) search system to a GenAI-supported system, analyzing search log data, survey data (n=25), and ten semi-structured interviews. Our findings show that adoption depended on the fit between the GenAI system's design assumptions and employees' work positionalities (role, spoken language, tenure). Further, we find that employees' trust in GenAI answers was built through source-checking, comparison among systems, and seeking input from colleagues or HR when in doubt. Our contribution is twofold. First, we provide empirical evidence of workplace GenAI adoption during a live organizational transition, showing that adoption is influenced by factors such as situational fit, search literacy, and trust calibration. It is also further shaped by knowledge conditions such as the system's content quality, employee training, and guidance. Second, we translate these findings into design considerations for inclusive deployment and adoption in high-stakes environments such as HR. We argue that organizations should design systems considering the role and context-sensitive benefits they yield to different social groups. They also need to treat the organizational knowledge infrastructure as AI infrastructure to improve the accountability and usability of GenAI systems

processed.summary:
- Evaluates the transition from legacy HR search systems to **GenAI-supported search**, highlighting the move toward **RAG-style internal knowledge retrieval**.
- Identifies that adoption is gated by **sociotechnical conditions**, where the technical architecture must align with diverse employee variables such as language, tenure, and role-specific needs.
- Establishes that **trust calibration** in GenAI is an active process involving **source-checking**, multi-system comparison, and HR-mediated verification, rather than passive acceptance.
- Reconceptualizes **organizational knowledge infrastructure** as a foundational component of **AI infrastructure**, asserting that data quality directly impacts system accountability.
key_results: Adoption rates correlate strongly with 'situational fit' between the AI’s design and the employee's specific work positionality.
Trust in GenAI outputs is non-linear and built through active validation and comparison with legacy systems.
Non-native English speakers and employees with shorter tenure face higher barriers to effective system utilization.
Search literacy and familiarity with prompt engineering are critical determinants of successful GenAI integration.
System content quality and institutional guidance are as important as the underlying model's reasoning capabilities.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers find that GenAI adoption in HR requires aligning system design with employee roles and organizational knowledge infrastructure.

title: AnchorKV: Safety-Aware KV Cache Compression via Soft Penalty with a Refusal Anchor
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) outperform earlier architectures on generative inference and long-context tasks, but their large size introduces significant challenges in memory usage, energy cost, and on-device deployment. Since scaling pre-trained language models improves downstream capability \cite{zhao2023survey}, the key-value (KV) cache becomes a dominant inference bottleneck. Recent KV cache compression methods \cite{jo2025fastkv,li2024snapkv,zhou2024dynamickv} reduce this cost by retaining only a subset of attention-relevant tokens. However, while these approaches preserve accuracy on benign workloads, their compression policies either fail to defend against jailbreak attacks \cite{jiang2024robustkv} or degrade safety alignment under aggressive eviction.
  We propose AnchorKV, a drop-in modification to KV cache compression that biases token retention scores away from directions in key space associated with harmful prompts. AnchorKV constructs an offline safety anchor by adapting a difference-of-means representation engineering approach \cite{arditi2024refusal,zou2023representation} to the layer-specific key projection space used in KV caching. Based on this anchor, a soft penalty token selection rule trades a small amount of utility for substantially improved safety alignment, while reducing to the original compressor when the penalty is zero.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **AnchorKV**, a safety-aware modification for **KV cache compression** designed to prevent safety alignment degradation and jailbreak vulnerabilities during token eviction.
* Utilizes **Representation Engineering** to construct an offline **safety anchor** within the layer-specific key projection space, identifying directions associated with harmful content.
* Employs a **soft penalty token selection** rule that biases retention scores away from malicious prompt components, allowing for a tunable trade-off between utility and security.
* Functions as a **drop-in modification** that can be integrated into existing compressors like SnapKV or DynamicKV to maintain robustness during aggressive pruning.
key_results: Identifies that standard KV eviction policies often inadvertently drop safety-critical tokens, leading to jailbreak susceptibility.
Adapts difference-of-means representation techniques specifically for the KV cache's key space.
Provides a mathematical framework for a soft penalty mechanism that reduces to standard compression when the penalty is zero.
Demonstrates significantly improved defense against adversarial prompts compared to traditional eviction methods.
Preserves model performance on benign downstream tasks while enforcing safety constraints during inference.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce AnchorKV to enhance LLM safety by applying a refusal-based soft penalty to KV cache compression.

title: StepGuard: Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration
summary: Web navigation requires agents to follow natural language goals, interact with web pages, and produce accurate answers. While recent advances leverage vision-language models and reinforcement learning, existing methods still suffer from single-step fragility due to reward misalignment and error propagation. To tackle the reward entanglement, we design Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO), which dynamically switches between a navigation-first mode for exploration and an answer-first mode for question-answering to mitigate reward conflict. To calibrate the single-step error, we propose Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR), a mechanism that estimates per-step confidence, triggers reflection only when necessary, and uses contrastive rewards to encourage self-correction to calibrate the single-step inaccuracy. With the above as the main components, we finally develop our StepGuard, a new framework of Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves navigation and answer accuracy, setting new state-of-the-art performance on standard web navigation benchmarks.

processed.summary:
- StepGuard introduces **Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO)**, which dynamically switches between navigation-first and answer-first modes to resolve reward entanglement.
- The framework features **Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR)**, a mechanism that triggers self-correction only when per-step confidence falls below a threshold.
- The system employs **contrastive rewards** during the reflection phase to calibrate single-step inaccuracies and prevent the accumulation of errors during long-horizon tasks.
- By integrating these components, the architecture effectively addresses **single-step fragility** and error propagation in Vision-Language Model (VLM) based agents.
key_results: Achieved new state-of-the-art performance across standard web navigation benchmarks.
Successfully decoupled navigation exploration from final question-answering objectives using DDPO.
Reduced redundant computations by triggering reflection only when necessary via confidence estimation.
Improved agent robustness against error propagation through per-step calibration.
Demonstrated superior answer accuracy compared to existing reinforcement learning and VLM approaches.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce StepGuard, a framework using dual-policy optimization and adaptive reflection to achieve SOTA web navigation.

title: A Quantitative Analysis of Multimodal Biomarkers in Alzheimer's Disease
summary: Despite increasing adoption of multimodal approaches in Alzheimer's Disease (AD) research -- aimed at integrating molecular, structural, clinical, and genetic biomarkers to enhance disease characterization -- the relationships among these modalities remain poorly understood. A systematic analysis of their dynamic interaction is essential for improving disease modeling, identifying redundant assessments, and reducing patient burden and acquisition costs. In this paper, we present a quantitative analysis of multimodal AD biomarkers by integrating tau-PET, structural MRI, cognitive scores (MMSE and CDR), and APOE4 data from 789 subjects drawn from the ADNI dataset. In our analyses, we (A) quantify cross-modal mutual information and explained variance to assess redundancy and predictive dependencies; (B) examine associations between tau topologies and structural atrophy across brain regions to select informative ROIs; (C) perform a statistical decomposition of the tau-cognition association into atrophy-related and atrophy-independent components; (D) and identify a dominant neurodegenerative trajectory that aligns with cognitive decline. This study provides a systematic characterization of cross-modal relationships, improving the interpretability and selection of biomarkers in AD. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/antonioscardace/Multimodal-AD.

processed.summary:
* The study introduces a framework for **multimodal biomarker integration**, utilizing tau-PET, structural MRI, and cognitive data to refine Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) characterization.
* Researchers utilized **mutual information** and **explained variance** metrics to quantify cross-modal redundancy, facilitating the selection of the most informative features.
* The methodology features a **statistical decomposition** that separates tau-related cognitive decline into atrophy-dependent and atrophy-independent paths for better interpretability.
* The framework maps a **neurodegenerative trajectory** using data from 789 subjects, providing a standardized approach for evaluating disease progression in medical AI models.
key_results: Quantified predictive dependencies between molecular tau-PET and structural MRI atrophy.
Mapped high-correlation Regions of Interest (ROIs) for targeted biomarker monitoring.
Successfully isolated the direct impact of tau pathology on cognitive decline from structural damage.
Identified a dominant neurodegenerative path that strongly correlates with MMSE and CDR scores.
Released a public code repository for cross-modal biomarker characterization and analysis.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Antonio Scardace and researchers introduce a quantitative multimodal framework to optimize biomarker selection and interpretability in Alzheimer's Disease.

title: GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine?
summary: Game generation is an emerging application of coding agents, requiring models to transform natural-language specifications into playable interactive systems. Unlike traditional coding tasks, game generation takes place within a game engine, where scripts, scenes, assets, rendering, and runtime interactions must jointly produce coherent gameplay. We formalize end-to-end game generation as the problem of producing a complete game artifact that realizes a specification through observable player-game interaction in a target environment. We argue that evaluating this setting requires three desiderata: Engine Grounding, Artifact Completeness, and Interactive Verification. We propose an interaction-grounded evaluation framework that assesses executable gameplay through replayed demonstrations and rubric-guided multimodal judging. We instantiate this framework as GameCraft-Bench, a benchmark comprising 140 Godot tasks across 15 game families. Evaluations of frontier coding agents show that end-to-end game generation remains highly challenging: the strongest agent achieves only 41.46%, and most agents score below 40%. Further analysis reveals that while agents often implement recognizable mechanics, they struggle to deliver complete games with sufficient content, functional visual feedback, and coherent presentation. See https://tongxuluo.github.io/gamecraft-bench-website for demos, code, and data.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **GameCraft-Bench**, the first benchmark designed to evaluate **LLM Agents** on end-to-end game development within the **Godot game engine**.
- Formalizes a framework centered on **Engine Grounding**, **Artifact Completeness**, and **Interactive Verification** to ensure generated games are playable and technically sound.
- Employs an **interaction-grounded evaluation** methodology using replayed demonstrations and **multimodal judging** to assess executable gameplay rather than just static code snippets.
- Analyzes the performance of frontier models, revealing significant gaps in their ability to synchronize **scripts, assets, and rendering** into a coherent player experience.
key_results: GameCraft-Bench comprises 140 complex Godot tasks spanning 15 diverse game families.
The top-performing frontier coding agent achieved a success rate of only 41.46%.
The majority of tested agents scored below 40%, indicating a high barrier for end-to-end game generation.
Agents often succeed at core mechanics but fail consistently at providing functional visual feedback.
Major bottlenecks identified include a lack of content volume and poor overall presentation coherence.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: HKU researchers introduce GameCraft-Bench, revealing that frontier agents currently struggle to build complete, interactive games in real engines.

title: An Epistemic Analysis of Random Coordinated Attack
summary:
The coordinated attack problem models the challenge of coordinating a joint action within a bounded time by communicating over unreliable links. It was the first distributed computing problem proven unsolvable. Its analysis also revealed the importance of common knowledge, a central concept in epistemic logic. However, the randomized version of coordinated attack, which is solvable, has not, to the best of our knowledge, been studied through the lens of probabilistic epistemic logic, where processes generate randomness by flipping coins.
  We present an epistemic logic framework for studying randomized algorithms that execute for a bounded number of rounds. The framework applies to coordinated attack, approximate agreement, and consensus, and supports dynamic graph models: synchronous systems in which reliable processes execute a bounded number of rounds while an adversary determines which messages are lost.
  Our approach combines techniques from the logical characterization of dynamic networks and task solvability with ideas from probabilistic dynamic epistemic logic. It is inspired by the operational model of Varghese and Lynch for randomized coordinated attack. More broadly, the resulting notion of probabilistic epistemic task solvability provides a foundation for the epistemic study of randomized distributed computation.
  Using this framework, we analyze the Varghese-Lynch algorithm from a knowledge-theoretic perspective, providing a formal treatment of the algorithm and its lower bound. As a byproduct, we strengthen the lower bound and show it is tight. The proof relies on indistinguishability arguments, demonstrating that reasoning about knowledge remains essential in the probabilistic setting. We also formalize the notion of information level introduced by Varghese and Lynch, showing that it corresponds to a specific epistemic formula.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a novel **probabilistic epistemic logic** framework for analyzing randomized distributed algorithms in synchronous systems with message loss.
• Bridges the gap between **dynamic graph models** and task solvability, specifically targeting the randomized version of the Coordinated Attack problem.
• Formalizes the **Varghese-Lynch algorithm**, using knowledge-theoretic perspectives to explain how processes reach consensus through coin-flipping.
• Analyzes the role of **common knowledge** and indistinguishability in probabilistic settings, providing a foundation for studying randomized distributed computation.
key_results: Development of an epistemic logic framework for bounded-round randomized algorithms.
Formalization and verification of the Varghese-Lynch algorithm for coordinated attack.
Derivation of a strengthened and tight lower bound for the coordination problem.
Demonstration that 'information level' corresponds to a specific epistemic formula.
Proof that reasoning about knowledge (indistinguishability) remains essential for probabilistic solvability.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers formalize randomized coordinated attack through epistemic logic to establish tighter bounds for distributed system coordination.

title: Meta-classification of one-class classification models using ranking correlation and nearest neighbor
summary: Machine Learning (ML) techniques have been applied to various problems. However, applying ML to ML models is an unexplored direction. For this purpose, this paper considers a meta-classification of one-class classification (OCC) models, because all ML models could be approximated as OCC models. The proposal represents OCC models as normality rankings and classifies them using nearest-neighbor and ranking-correlation metrics. The experiment classifies OCC models, where classes correspond to training datasets, algorithms, and hyperparameters. The proposal achieves high accuracy when class labels are datasets. Moreover, it can classify algorithms when the training datasets contain the same class. In addition, the discussion highlights that the classification of OCC models is essentially the classification of datasets that treats multiple samples as a single input. The experiment demonstrates the classification of datasets using sleeping records. The proposed method can provide a unified solution for classifying OCC models, datasets, and rankings. Source code is uploaded to the public repository https://github.com/ToshiHayashi/ClassOCC.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a novel **meta-classification framework** that treats One-Class Classification (OCC) models as primary data points by representing them as **normality rankings**. 
 • Employs **nearest-neighbor classification** integrated with **ranking-correlation metrics** to identify model characteristics such as training datasets, algorithms, and hyperparameters. 
 • Establishes a theoretical and practical bridge where model classification serves as a proxy for **complex dataset classification**, treating multiple samples as a single input unit. 
 • Provides a unified solution for **AI Evaluation** and model fingerprinting, potentially improving how we distinguish between variations in model training regimes.
key_results: Achieved high accuracy in identifying the specific training datasets used to generate OCC models.
Successfully classified distinct algorithms when models were trained on datasets from the same class.
Demonstrated the method's efficacy in classifying complex temporal datasets, such as sleeping records.
Validated ranking-correlation metrics as robust distance measures for comparing model behavior.
Released the ClassOCC public repository to enable reproducible meta-classification of ML models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ToshiHayashi introduces a meta-classification method using normality rankings and correlation metrics to identify the datasets and algorithms behind ML models.
